news_communities.title: Malden woman accused of threatening federal agents making immigration arrest
news_communities.author: Thea DiGiammerino
news_communities.summary:
A Massachusetts woman is accused of threatening to kill federal agents who were conducting immigration enforcement in Malden.

Bethany Abigail Terrill, 37, was charged with threatening a United States official, the U.S. Attorney’s Office said Thursday.

Prosecutors say that Terrill approached the agents as they were making an arrest outside Malden District Court on Sept. 29, describing her as verbally abusive. She allegedly shouted things like “ICE is here, ICE is here,” “You guys are monsters, this is insane,” “Sir, what’s your name, what’s your name,” “I can try to help you,” and “I am an American civilian, I have a right to be here,” and called the agents Nazis.

She is further accused of trying to push past the agents, yelling “Charlie Kirk died, and we love it… We’re coming for you, gonna kill you.” 

According to the statement of probable cause, an agent heard the threat and proceeded to detain Terrill, who they said was “actively resistant and fought to escape.”

Officials say the agents were wearing body cameras, and Terrill was also filming at the time. In the statement of probable cause, investigators say the threat can be heard in the footage from Terrill’s phone.

The agents on scene were from multiple agencies, including Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), ICE and the FBI.

During her appearance in U.S. District Court in Boston on Thursday, a public defender said Terrill owns a salon and was struggling with legal issues in an unrelated case.

Judge Paul Levenson ordered Terrill be released with conditions, saying “federal agents have a right to not be subjected to threats.”

Terrill was ordered as part of her conditions of release to participate in a mental health program for treatment on a regular basis, to develop a safety plan for her own safety and others, avoid contact with potential witnesses, have no contact with any federal official, family or otherwise in any way or form, refrain from alcohol and the use of any illegal drugs, to avoid contact with law enforcement, and to refrain from making any further threats.

She didn’t speak to reporters outside of court.

The charge of threatening a U.S. official carries a sentence of up to 10 years in prison, three years of supervised release and a fine of $250,000.

news_communities.title: Cities bordering Everett unite in Revolution soccer stadium transparency push
news_communities.author: Asher Klein
news_communities.summary:
Four cities that border Everett, Massachusetts, have banded together in a push for more involvement in the New England Revolution’s plans for a soccer-specific stadium in the city.

The mayors of Revere, Medford, Malden and Chelsea formed a coalition called City Mayors for Citizens that they said will seek transparency on plans for the stadium, including the impacts it will have on their communities.

The 25,000-seat stadium proposal has progressed in the state Legislature but still faces plenty of hurdles. The City Mayors for Citizens said they are worried about how the building on along the Mystic River might impact local infrastructure, public transit and quality of life.

“A project of this size and scale will have significant regional impacts,” said Revere Mayor Patrick Keefe Jr said in a statement. “We want to ensure that a stadium is contributing positively to the communities it is drawing resources from. Our residents should not bear the burdens of a private stadium that has strong potential to negatively affect their day-to-day lives.”

Asked for a response to the statement, a Revolution club spokesperson noted that it expects to work with neighboring communities after it reaches agreements with Everett and Boston, which would allow the property to have its port designation removed and the process of construction to continue.

The spokesperson also noted that the team thanked the mayors and promised to review the concerns raised in their letter, sent Monday.

news_communities.title: Man shot in Saugus road rage incident
news_communities.summary:
A man was shot and injured Wednesday evening in a road rage incident in Saugus, Massachusetts, according to police.

Officers responded shortly after 8 p.m. to the reported shooting on Lincoln Avenue in Cliftondale Square. The incident happened outside Banana Splitz, an ice cream shop.

The victim, a 32-year-old man from Melrose, was found shot in the leg, police said. He suffered a serious but non-life-threatening leg injury.

Police arrested Stephen Cooper, a 48-year-old Saugus man, who remained at the scene of the shooting.

Authorities did not specify charges against Cooper, a licensed firearm carrier, but noted that he is facing several gun charges.

“His license to carry is pending suspension by the licensing authority,” a Saugus police spokesperson said in a statement.

No further details were immediately available.

news_communities.title: Video shows Salvadoran man being detained by ICE in Malden
news_communities.author: Oscar Margain, Marc Fortier and Mark Garfinkel
news_communities.summary:
The wife of a Salvadoran man says he was violently taken into custody by immigration officials on his way to work in Malden, Massachusetts, on Wednesday morning.

The incident occurred shortly before 6 a.m. in the area of 28 Kennedy Drive.

Leslie Perlera Gonzalez said she received a distressing call from her husband, Edgar Hernan Elias Escobar, shortly before 6 a.m. as he was heading to his construction job. She rushed to Kennedy Drive, where she said Elias Escobar was surrounded by immigration officers.

“I heard them smash his window, and he was saying, ‘Ow, you’re hurting me,'” Perlera Gonzalez said. “When I arrived on the scene, he had a Taser on his face.”

The 31-year-old had no criminal record and was in the process of adjusting his immigration status after the couple married last year, noted Perlera Gonzalez.

“He’s never come close to doing anything that would be considered a crime,” she said.

She alleged that agents used an unmarked car to ram her husband’s vehicle, forcing him off the road before detaining him.

Malden police said Elias Escobar sustained minor injuries. He was transported on a stretcher to a hospital in Melrose, where Perlera Gonzalez said she was not allowed to see him.

About an hour later, he was moved to a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement processing facility in Burlington.

A spokesperson from the Department of Homeland Security said in a statement that Elias Escobar was the target of the ICE operation and suffered “no injuries.”

“On September 17, 2025, ICE conducted an immigration enforcement operation in Malden, Massachusetts to arrest Edgar Hernan Elias-Escobar, an illegal alien from El Salvador. During the arrest, the illegal alien refused to comply with officer commands to rolldown his window. The officers took appropriate action and followed their training to use the minimum amount of force necessary to resolve the situation in a manner that ensured the success of the operation and prioritized the safety of the public and our officers,” the spokesperson said in a statement. “Out of an abundance of caution, ICE law enforcement escorted Elias-Escobar to the hospital for evaluations. Medical staff found he had no injuries and discharged him. He was then escorted to the Boston Filed office for processing and will be placed in removal proceedings.”

“I’ve been expecting this from somebody I know every day since Donald Trump was elected,” said Amiee Parco, an attorney with Greater Boston Legal Services and a colleague of Perlera Gonzalez. “I’ve been waiting for it to affect me personally.”

Friends and coworkers gathered outside the Burlington facility, joining a weekly protest to support Elias Escobar’s release and that of other detainees.

“He’s a really wonderful person,” said Alyssa Rao, a friend of the couple. “I think this is going to impact everyone.”

After Wednesday’s ordeal, Perlera Gonzalez fears what her husband is going through while detained.

“I’m terrified of what could be happening when no one is watching,” she said.

At last check, Escobar was still at the facility. It’s unclear how long he’ll be held — often, people stay several days, though the facility is meant to detain people for hours.

His lawyer is trying to prevent the government from sending him to a far-away detention center, and from deportation.

ICE has ramped up arrests across greater Boston in recent weeks under “Operation Patriot 2.0,” detaining immigrants in public spaces.

Gov. Maura Healey spoke out against the sweeps on Tuesday, saying federal immigration enforcement officers are “creating an incredible amount of fear and intimidation.”

“Some of it seems purposeful, to be honest. Why else are you parked outside a church? Why are you parked outside a community center?”

The governor said her office has received no information from ICE about who is being detained or why.

“This is not a furtherance of public safety,” she said. “In fact, it undermines public safety.”

Asked what she can do to protest White House policy, Healey said: “I join with other governors in urging the Trump administration, in the strongest possible terms, to change course.”

State House News Service contributed to this report.

news_boston.title: NH Supreme Court overturns Adam Montgomery's murder conviction in daughter's death
news_boston.author: Marc Fortier
news_boston.summary:
The New Hampshire Supreme Court has overturned Adam Montgomery’s murder conviction for the 2019 death of his 5-year-old daughter Harmony.

Montgomery was convicted of second-degree murder and second-degree assault in 2024 and sentenced to 56 years to life in prison in connection with his daughter’s death, but the Supreme Court overturned the murder charge Thursday, saying that keeping the two charges together jeopardized his right to a fair trial.

[READ THE FULL RULING HERE]

The court affirmed Montgomery’s convictions for assault, falsifying physical evidence, witness tampering and abusing Harmony’s corpse.

“Although the defendant requests that we reverse all his convictions, his brief does not specifically address his convictions of falsifying physical evidence, witness tampering, and abuse of a corpse. To the extent the defendant does challenge those convictions on appeal, he has failed to demonstrate reversible error,” the justices wrote in their ruling. “For the foregoing reasons, we reverse the defendant’s conviction of second degree murder, affirm his convictions of second degree assault, falsifying physical evidence, witness tampering, and abuse of a corpse, and remand.”

The New Hampshire Supreme Court heard Montgomery’s appeal in October.

His lawyer, Pamela Phelan, argued at the time that the assault case never should have been combined with the murder case. The defense believed the assault charges, tied to an incident in July 2019, would be approached separately from the murder charge, which alleged events on Dec. 7, 2019. But the defense said the prosecution used the assault cause to establish a larger pattern of abuse. During the trial, the defense requested the cases be severed, but that request was denied.

This is tied to a larger defense argument that the court should not have admitted “bad acts” evidence — evidence that Adam Montgomery assaulted and neglected Harmony in the two weeks leading up to her death, and that he prevented her mother, Crystal Corey, from seeing her daughter to cover up that abuse.

Investigators believe Harmony was killed nearly two years before she was reported missing in 2021. Her body has never been found. Investigators believe Harmony’s remains are somewhere along a 26-mile route that Adam Montgomery took with a rental truck into Massachusetts.

news_boston.title: Protesters arrested at Boston City Hall after causing chaos at meeting
news_boston.author: Kaitlin McKinley Becker
news_boston.summary:
A group of 15 to 20 protesters allegedly created a loud and lengthy disturbance at a Boston City Hall meeting on Wednesday, bringing the meeting to a halt and resulting in multiple arrests.

Boston police say ultimately seven people, ranging in age from 24 to 82, were arrested for trespass following the coordinated disruption that began around 2:50 p.m. An eighth person was not arrested on scene, however officers were going to seek an application for criminal complaint at Boston Municipal Court to charge a 28-year-old woman, also with trespass.

The group had been seated in the public gallery of the Iannella Chamber, according to police, when they rose in unison shortly before 3 p.m., unfurled signs bearing political messages, crossed into the restricted area of the chamber, and began loudly chanting and waving signs.

City Council President Liz Breadon, who was presiding over the meeting, repeatedly called the chamber to order but was unable to restore decorum due to the level of noise, police added.

Officers responsible for the security of City Hall and its occupants who were present in the chamber requested additional Boston police officers to help with the rapidly evolving situation, and a decision was made in consultation with city council staff to clear all bystanders from the chamber.

Leadership from the property management department, which oversees building management at City Hall and has the authority to issue trespass notices, was on scene and, following additional discussions with city council stuff, determined that the remaining demonstrators were not permitted to stay within the chamber due to their active disruption of a public meeting. Those who remained were advised they would be considered trespassing if they refused to leave voluntarily.

Several elected officials volunteered to serve as intermediaries, according to police, as officers at first chose not to directly engage the demonstrators in the interest of de-escalation. About half of the protesters chose to voluntarily leave after those negotiations, and they were escorted out of the building without further incident.

The remaining eight people in the group allegedly refused to leave, laying on the floor and linking their arms. They were informed multiple times by city council staff and city officials that they were required to vacate the premises or face arrest, police said. Negotiations were allowed to continue for about another 30 minutes before it was determined that the group was not going to leave willingly.

Officers then made a formal verbal announcement, in the presence of a representative from the property management department, informing each of the eight remaining protesters that they were no longer welcome in City Hall, police said. Officers spoke with each person individually, confirmed they understood the announcement, and offered each a final opportunity to leave.

Each individual indicated they understood but remained on the floor, according to police. Seven people were taken into custody for trespass at that point, including: George Lee Jr., 43, of Dorchester; Jonathan M. Barry, 40, of Roslindale; Linda A. Jenkins, 77, of Malden; Nancy Ellen Griffin, 59; Vikiana Petit-Homme, 24; Margaret Hughes, 32; and Rebecca A. Pierce, 82, of Dorchester.

Police did not release the name of the eighth person they were seeking to charge with trespass.

Details weren’t immediately shared on what the group was protesting, or when they may be due in court.

news_boston.title: How family sacrifices motivated USMNT star Ricardo Pepi to reach the World Cup
news_boston.author: Mike Gavin
news_boston.summary:
Ricardo Pepi was competing in a soccer match when it began to rain.

The future U.S. men’s national team star, just a teenager at the time, continued playing as the rain fell and the stands emptied. One soaked spectator, however, remained seated. His father.  

“No one around him, the rain was falling down hard and he’s watching his son,” Miguel López, Pepi’s former coach, recalled on NBC’s “My New Favorite Futbolista.” “That gave me a lot of respect for him.”

It was a small but symbolic moment shared between Pepi, his father and Mother Nature that encapsulated the family’s dedication to the pursuit of a dream. It showed that no matter where Pepi’s soccer career took him, his family would always be there — near or far, win or lose, rain or shine.

“The most important thing is the sacrifices you have to make to get here,” said Pepi, now a 23-year-old striker for PSV Eindhoven in the Netherlands. “Not only mine, but also my family’s — my parents, my brothers.”

Those early sacrifices began while Pepi was growing up in El Paso, Texas — an area along the Mexico border where, even at a young age, soccer isn’t exactly played for fun.  

“No matter what club it is, even for 5-, 6- or 7-year-old kids, here you play to win,” López said. “Ricardo was one of those neighborhood kids who had a ball with him every day, at any hour.”

And his family was always nearby, making the long drives for training sessions and games. They were the anchors of his support system, his mother providing the comforts of home and his father roaming the sidelines.

“My dad was very important for my career,” Pepi said. “He was my coach for 10 or 11 years. That takes time. That takes sacrifice.”

Like many kids, Pepi eventually left home while pursuing a career. Unlike many kids, Pepi was only 13 years old when he did so.

My New Favorite Futbolista

My New Favorite Futbolista Jun 9

Why Cristian Roldan chose to play for USMNT at World Cup over 2 other countries

My New Favorite Futbolista Jun 4

Canada goalkeeper Dayne St. Clair hopes to fulfill a childhood goal at the World Cup

My New Favorite Futbolista Jun 2

Why U.S. men's soccer captain Tim Ream uses LEGO sets to manage mental health

FC Dallas offered him the chance to continue developing in their system. That initially meant moving more than 600 miles and nine hours away from his family, until they were able to join him.

“If you have kids, you know what it means to let a son go alone, at 13 years old, to another big city to fulfill his dream,” López said. “Yes, it is very difficult.”

It was just as difficult for Pepi, who was thrust into new surroundings without the nearby support of his family.

“I had plenty of time to think about the sacrifices I had made in leaving my family,” Pepi said. “But I think it was the moment when I realized all the things my family has done for me to be where I am now.”

The family eventually joined Pepi in Dallas, and it was during that time that his father watched him play in the rain. Seeing his family’s sacrifices further motivated Pepi to make his dream become a reality.

When he was 18 years old, he had a breakout year in Major League Soccer with 13 goals in 31 games, en route to being named MLS Young Player of the Year.

Pepi had an opportunity to represent his home country when he made his USMNT debut in 2021, scoring in his first international appearance to become the team’s eighth-youngest goalscorer.

He then made the leap to Europe in record-breaking fashion. FC Dallas transferred him to FC Augsburg in Germany in January 2022 for what was the highest transfer fee for a homegrown player in MLS history.

Success brought more change and challenges.

“The first six months were very difficult obviously,” Pepi said. “I was in a different culture, a different country, speaking a different language.”

And learning how to do his own laundry.

“Life is very easy when your mother is at home,” he said.

The lessons he learned on and off the pitch proved to be invaluable.

“Going to Europe young was one of the best things that could have happened to him,” López said. “So he could absorb the training and the soccer culture over there, which is very different from what it is here.”

It prepared him for what he thought would be his first appearance in the World Cup in 2022. But when U.S. Soccer announced its 26-player World Cup roster, Pepi was not on it. That disappointment was shared not only between Pepi and his family, but throughout the El Paso community.

“It’s a dream for me, it’s also a dream for them,” he said. “It was difficult for everyone.”

Pepi said not being named to the World Cup helped him mature and grow as a player, making him strive for the next opportunity.  

“My parents told me that everything happens for a reason, that I should keep working,” he said. “‘Maybe it’s not the World Cup you were supposed to play in.’”

His journey to the World Cup — with stops along the way in El Paso, Dallas, Germany and the Netherlands — would be completed four years later.  

Pepi has recorded 13 goals in 37 caps for the national team, making him one of the most prolific U.S. strikers of his generation. That helped ensure that his name would be called when the 2026 World Cup roster was announced in May.

He’ll make his World Cup debut on June 12 when the U.S. takes on Paraguay in Los Angeles — his presence on the sport’s grandest stage a tribute to all those who made sacrifices to help get him there.    

“We have all contributed, his first coach, his parents, his school teachers, the equipment managers, everyone,” López said. “And I think they are very proud of what he has done and what he will continue to do in the future.”

They’ll be there to watch, even if it’s raining.

You can tune into “My New Favorite Futbolista” wherever you listen to podcasts, with episodes featuring Pepi and more World Cup stars.
